title: Bridge Financing Explained: Buy Before You Sell
metaTitle: Bridge Financing in Canada — How Bridge Loans Work
metaDescription: Bridge financing in Canada: how bridge loans work, what they cost, and when they make sense. For Greater Vancouver buyers managing back-to-back closings.
excerpt: Closing on the new home before the old one sells? Bridge financing makes the math work. Here's exactly how it functions.

body.heading: What a bridge loan does
paragraphs: A bridge loan is short-term financing — typically 30 to 120 days — secured against your existing home's equity. It covers the down-payment gap when you close on the new property before the old one's sale completes. Once the existing home sells, the bridge is repaid in full from the sale proceeds.

body.heading: What it costs
paragraphs: Rate: prime plus 2–4% (around 8–10% in 2026). Setup fee: $300–$500. There's typically no monthly payment during the bridge — interest accrues and is paid out of sale proceeds. On a $300K bridge for 45 days at 9%, total cost is about $3,300. Cheap, given the alternative is making your offer subject-to-sale (which weakens the offer significantly in any competitive Greater Vancouver market).

body.heading: How to qualify
paragraphs: Most lenders require your existing home to be firm-sold — subjects removed, deposit in lawyer's trust — before they'll fund the bridge. A few will fund 'open bridge' against an unsold listing, at higher cost. The bridge is usually tied to the new mortgage at the same lender; arranging it is a clause in the new mortgage commitment, not a separate application.

faqs.question: What if my existing home doesn't sell during the bridge?
faqs.answer: Most bridges can be extended for an additional 30–60 days at the same rate. Past that, the bridge converts to a different product (typically a HELOC or second mortgage) at higher cost. Plan with a realistic timeline.

faqs.question: Do I need to use the same lender for both?
faqs.answer: Almost always yes. Bridges are usually offered as part of the new mortgage commitment — separate-lender bridges are rare and pricier.
